title: Adding alt text and heading structure
what_it_is: Alt text is a short written description added to an image, so screen reading software can describe it. Headings are the built-in title styles — Heading 1, Heading 2 and so on — used to structure a document so it isn't just plain, unstructured text.
why_use_it: Together, alt text and headings let someone using a screen reader — software that reads the screen aloud — understand an image and move through a document by its structure, instead of having everything read out in one long, flat block.
how_to: To add alt text in Word, PowerPoint or Outlook: right-click the image and select Edit Alt Text (or View Alt Text, depending on the app).
Type a short, plain description of what the image shows and what it's for — for example "Bar chart showing attendance by month" rather than "image1.jpg".
If an image is purely decorative and adds no information, tick the Mark as decorative box instead of writing a description.

To add heading structure in Word: select the text you want to use as a heading, then go to the Home tab and select Heading 1 for a main title, or Heading 2, Heading 3 and so on for sections within it.
Use headings in order — don't skip from Heading 1 straight to Heading 3 — so the structure makes sense to someone navigating by heading alone.
Avoid making text look like a heading just by making it bold or bigger — use the actual Heading style, or screen reading software won't recognise it as one.
